6 min read
4 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Recently, we rewrote one of our frontend features from scratch. This was not a small cleanup or a cosmetic refactoring. It was a full replacement of the old implementation using AI-assisted SDLC, SpecKit, GitHub Copilot, and Claude Opus.
The feature was responsible for mapping imported data on the frontend. It worked, but over time the code became hard to read, hard to change, and even harder to debug. Every defect in this area looked like a small fix, but in practice, it could easily create a new regression.
So instead of continuing with endless patching, we decided to first describe the expected behavior in a specification, split it into user stories, and only then replace the old implementation with a new one based on that specification.
AI really helped speed up the implementation, but it didn’t do all the work for us. The most important parts were the specification, isolated frontend architecture, and a proper review process.

What is SpecKit
For this refactoring, I used SpecKit from GitHub.
SpecKit is a set of tools and an approach for Spec-Driven Development. The main idea is simple: do not start with a chaotic prompt like “rewrite this feature for me”. First, turn the requirements into a clear structure. Describe the behavior, scenarios, constraints, acceptance criteria, and only then move to implementation.
For AI-assisted development, this is critical. GitHub Copilot or Claude can generate code quickly, but the quality of the result depends directly on the context. If the context is unclear, you can get code that looks correct but does not solve the right problem.
In my case, SpecKit helped move the work from “let’s try to generate something” to a more controlled process:
specification -> user stories -> implementation
Technical context
The project is built with Angular 19. On the frontend, we use our own modular architecture with isolated modules.
One of the key rules in this architecture is that modules should not depend on each other directly.
In everyday development, this rule can sometimes feel like extra work. You need to think about where to put the code, how to define module boundaries, and how to avoid unnecessary coupling. But during this refactoring, that effort paid off very quickly.
The old feature was isolated enough, so we were able to remove it without a domino effect across the whole application. We did not need to untangle a long chain of dependencies across several modules or worry that a local change would break something in a completely different part of the product.

Why we needed refactoring
The old implementation did its job, but it was becoming harder and harder to maintain.
The problem was not that we simply “did not like the code”. The real problem was that we had lost confidence in our understanding of it. Every change required a lot of time to investigate the context. And after a fix, there was still a feeling that something else could be broken nearby.
And that is exactly what happened. We fixed one defect and got a regression issue. Sometimes more than one. Over time, this part of the system became an area where every change either broke something or carried a high risk of a new defect.
At some point, it became clear: continuing to patch the old implementation was just delaying the problem. We needed a new implementation that was easier to read, understand, and change.

Why the specification became more important than the old code
Initially, I approached this as a standard AI-assisted refactoring. Armed with the legacy code, Copilot, and Claude Opus, taking it step by step seemed feasible.
However, we ran into an issue almost immediately.
When I started creating the new specification, the AI agent kept trying to use the existing implementation as a reference. This made sense: the old code was right there and looked like a ready source of context. But that code was also the source of the problem.
So I changed the approach. I removed the legacy code — the whole module with the feature — so AI would not use it as a reference. The specification became the base: what the feature should do, what scenarios it should support, what states it should handle, and what result we expected.
The key change was this: not “refactor the old code”, but “implement the feature from scratch based on the specification”.

How the process was organized
The work was built around AI-assisted SDLC and SpecKit.
The process looked like this:

• Analyzing the problems in the old implementation.

• Defining the expected behavior of the feature.

• Drafting the specification.

• Splitting it into user stories and adding them to Jira.

• Generating the core implementation using GitHub Copilot and Claude Opus.

• Creating a separate merge request for each user story.

• Running code review.

• Merging the changes one by one.

• Sending the feature to testing.
Coding was the fastest part of the work. Once the specification was clear enough, AI helped build the main implementation quite quickly.
More time went into writing the specification and preparing the process: clarifying details, splitting the work into tasks, and checking that we were moving in the right direction.

Delivery with chained merge requests
We did not want to create one huge merge request that would be hard to review. So we split the implementation into several user stories.
That created a new challenge: these stories depended on each other. If each story targeted master directly, we would have to wait until the first user story was merged before properly moving on to the next one.
So we used chained merge requests:
Story 1 -> master
Story 2 -> Story 1 branch
Story 3 -> Story 2 branch
Story 4 -> Story 3 branchThis allowed us to prepare several parts of the work in parallel and speed up development while the first story was going through review and merge. Reviewers could check smaller, logically separated changes, while we could keep moving forward.
After approval, we merged everything step by step:
merge Story 1
merge Story 2
merge Story 3
merge Story 4This approach worked well, but it came with trade-offs.
The main downside of chained merge requests is maintaining the chain. If something important changes in the first story after review, it can affect all the next branches. Then you need to rebase, resolve conflicts, and check that the logic in the next MRs is still correct.
Another challenge is review clarity. Review can become confusing if reviewers do not pay attention to the target branch. Some changes from previous stories can appear in the next MRs, making it harder to understand what exactly needs to be reviewed in that specific part.
So chained MRs are not a silver bullet. They give speed and better reviewability, but they also require effort: clean commits, clear MR descriptions, correct target branches, and regular syncing between branches.
In our case, this trade-off was worth it. We avoided one huge MR and moved faster without losing control over delivery.

What took the most time
Implementation was faster than I expected. The main part of the code was generated and brought to a working state quite quickly.
But two things took much more time: the specification and UI polish.
The specification part was expected. To get a good result from AI, you first need to understand what exactly should be implemented. You cannot just ask AI to “think for you”. If the requirements are unclear, the result will also be unclear.
The second difficult part was styling and matching the design. The last user story was mostly about UI: spacing, alignment, states, and small visual details. AI helped with the basic structure, but the final polish was still manual work.
UI polish and styles took about 8 hours. This is a good reminder for frontend development: AI can quickly generate business logic, but pixel-level quality still needs a careful engineer with the design open nearby.

Results in numbers
Metric Value
------------------------------------------
User stories 9
Merge requests 9
Removed legacy files 19
Added new files 16
LOC before refactoring ~1700
LOC after refactoring ~1300
Time spent on specification ~16h
Time spent on UI polish / styles ~8h
Defects after QA 2
Time spent on fixes after QA ~16h
Main takeaway
This refactoring was not about “AI writing the code for us”. It showed something else: AI can be very effective inside a normal engineering process, but it does not replace the process itself.
The main takeaway is simple: AI agents do not remove the need for engineering discipline. In fact, they make it even more important. If there are no clear module boundaries, AI can quickly spread complexity across the whole project. If there is no specification, it can quickly generate a new version of the old problem. If there is no review and testing, speed only increases the risk.
AI is an accelerator. But the direction, boundaries, and responsibility for the result still belong to the engineer.
